text: This video presents a wide local excision robotic prostatectomy for high-risk prostate cancer. The components of a wide local excision are an intact non-VA fascia with perirectal fat, an extended lymph node dissection boundary that includes all nodal tissue between the internal and external iliac vessels and surrounding the obturator nerve with proximal dissection to the common iliac bifurcation, a wide posterior lateral prostatic margin, and a wide bladder neck excision and reconstruction. Here are MRIs of patients in whom we've performed wide local excision. This MRI shows high burden disease with left extracapsular extension. This MRI shows left seminal vesicle involvement. We've performed approximately twenty wide local excisions, and what follows is a representative video of a few.

The surgeon uses a posterior approach to incise along the posterior cul-de-sac, immediately identifying the non-vious fascia and dissecting down to the longitudinal fibers of the rectum. The lateral aspects are skeletonized, exposing the endopelvic fascia. Dissection continues down to the levator ani muscles on both the left and right sides.

The surgeon begins dissection of the seminal vesicles and vas deferens, identifying and sharply transecting the lateral aspect of the vas deferens to provide traction. The lateral pedicle of the seminal vesicle is clipped and transected, followed by anterior dissection along the seminal vesicle. The dissection stops when detrusor fibers become visible, ensuring good tissue coverage of the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon performs an extended pelvic lymph node dissection, removing nodal tissue between the internal and external iliac vessels and around the obturator nerve, up to the common iliac bifurcation. The lateral edge of the rectum is visible. A standard stitch is used to ligate the dorsal venous complex, and a wide incision is made at the bladder neck for clear visualization of the ureteral orifices.
The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to lift the prostate, rotating the bladder neck into view. The left and right ureter orifices are clearly visible during the bladder neck reconstruction.

The surgeon maintains a wide posterolateral margin at the prostatic pedicles, ensuring all periprostatic tissue is dissected off with the prostate. The apical dissection is performed using standard techniques, with visible separation of tissue layers.

The surgeon performs the bladder anastomosis using a double-armed V-lock suture. The final specimen reveals a wide bladder neck excision, wide posterolateral margins, and intact fascia. The specimen includes perirectal fat and extended lymph node dissection, demonstrating wide excision and reconstruction.
